_meta.source: FAA Office of Spaceports list (faa.gov/space/spaceports_by_state) + per-site Wikipedia coordinate verification

license_categories.commercial: FAA Launch Site Operator License (LSO/LRSO) or Reentry Site Operator (RSO)
license_categories.federal: Federal range — DoD/NASA, no FAA spaceport license required
license_categories.private: Private exclusive-use; vehicle-specific FAA launch licenses, no spaceport operator license

sites.name: Pacific Spaceport Complex – Alaska
sites.activity: Three vertical launch pads on Kodiak Island serving small-lift orbital and suborbital vehicles. Active commercial customers include ABL Space (RS1), Astra (formerly Rocket 3), and U.S. DoD missile-defense interceptors. Operations were suspended 2014–2016 after a Strategic Target System failure damaged Pad 1; the complex resumed launches in 2018 and has flown on average 2–4 missions per year since.

sites.name: Mojave Air & Space Port
sites.activity: First U.S. facility licensed for horizontal launches of reusable spacecraft (2004). Tenants include Virgin Galactic flight ops, Scaled Composites, Stratolaunch (Roc carrier aircraft), Masten (now Astrobotic) and a long roster of suborbital and aircraft test programs. Mojave does not host vertical orbital launches; it is a high-desert flight-test and aerospace-MRO hub at 2,801 ft elevation.

sites.name: Vandenberg Space Force Base
sites.activity: Primary U.S. west-coast launch range for polar and sun-synchronous orbits. SpaceX flies Falcon 9 from SLC-4E (high-cadence Starlink, NRO, NASA payloads); ULA flies Vulcan from SLC-3E and Atlas V legacy missions; Firefly launches Alpha from SLC-2W. SLC-6 is being converted by SpaceX for west-coast Falcon Heavy from 2025–2026. The 99,000-acre coastal range also supports DoD missile testing.

sites.name: Colorado Air & Space Port
sites.activity: Horizontal-takeoff spaceport east of Denver, certified for runway-launched suborbital and point-to-point vehicles. No orbital launches to date; current tenants are flight-test and engine-development programs. In 2025 Boom Technology took over the former Reaction Engines facility on-site to test supersonic propulsion. Otherwise the field functions as a busy general-aviation airport with the tallest GA control tower in the U.S.

sites.name: Space Florida Launch Complex 46
sites.activity: Small-lift vertical pad on Cape Canaveral SFS, leased by Space Florida from the U.S. Air Force/Navy and offered to commercial tenants. Hosted Astra Rocket 3 (LV0008, Feb 2022, partial failure), Athena/LM-100 (1990s), and Trident D5 development. Currently configured to support multiple small-vehicle providers including Phantom Space and Vaya Space.

sites.name: Space Florida Launch & Landing Facility (former Shuttle Landing Facility)
sites.activity: The 15,000 ft former shuttle runway on Merritt Island, now operated as a horizontal launch and orbital-reentry site. Sierra Space targets Dream Chaser landings here; Amazon Leo (formerly Project Kuiper) runs a $120 M satellite-processing facility on the apron. Over 3,200 flight ops and 340 landings supported in 2025.

sites.name: Cecil Air & Space Port
sites.activity: Horizontal-launch spaceport in Jacksonville with 12,503 ft runway, hosting flight-test, MRO, Florida Air National Guard, and U.S. Coast Guard ops. No orbital launches to date. In Sept 2024 ground broke on a hypersonic-aircraft test facility for transatlantic-class vehicles; that program plus general aviation and corporate flight ops dominate current activity.

sites.name: Space Coast Regional Airport
sites.activity: Public regional airport in Titusville licensed for horizontal launches. No orbital launches to date; the field hosts Bristow Academy helicopter flight school (~350 pilots/year), the Valiant Air Command Warbird Museum, NASA/KSC personnel transport, and corporate aviation. Renewed by the FAA in 2025; license valid through 2030.

sites.name: Cape Canaveral Space Force Station
sites.activity: America's busiest launch range. SpaceX flies Falcon 9 from SLC-40 (Starlink, Crew/Cargo Dragon, NASA, NRO) at >100 launches/year cadence; ULA flies Vulcan and Atlas V from SLC-41; Falcon Heavy uses LC-39A on adjacent KSC. Blue Origin is preparing LC-36 for New Glenn. Multiple booster-landing zones (LZ-1/2) on the cape itself.

sites.name: Kennedy Space Center (LC-39A/B)
sites.activity: LC-39A is leased by SpaceX for Falcon 9 (Crew Dragon) and Falcon Heavy, plus East-Coast Starship (under construction). LC-39B hosts NASA's Space Launch System; Artemis II is planned for April 2026. The center includes the 525 ft Vehicle Assembly Building, two crawler-transporters, and the operational Mobile Launchers.

sites.name: Spaceport Camden
sites.activity: Vertical small-orbital launch site authorized for up to 12 launches, 12 static fires, and 12 wet-dress rehearsals per year, with azimuths 83–115° threading between Cumberland Island National Seashore and offshore. Site activity has been almost entirely legal/environmental since licensure; Camden County voters rejected the project's continued bond funding in March 2022 and FAA license remains active but unused. License expires Dec 20 2026.

sites.name: Spaceport America
sites.activity: World's first purpose-built commercial spaceport. Anchor tenant Virgin Galactic flies VSS Unity/VMS Eve (suborbital) from the 12,000 ft runway. UP Aerospace flies SpaceLoft sounding rockets from the vertical area; SpinLaunch operates a kinetic-launch test mass driver on-site. Heavy 2024–2026 Virgin Galactic Delta-class vehicle ramp; FAA license valid through 2028.

sites.name: Oklahoma Air & Space Port (Burns Flat)
sites.activity: Former Clinton-Sherman AFB; 13,500 ft runway, 5,000 sq mi of restricted airspace overhead. Licensed for horizontal launch but no orbital missions to date. Used as flight-test field for Burt Rutan Boomerang, USAF UAV testing, and X-Hab/atmospheric research. License renewed June 2021 and currently up for renewal in 2026.

sites.name: Houston Spaceport (Ellington)
sites.activity: 400-acre commercial campus on Ellington Airport, the 10th licensed U.S. spaceport. Anchor tenants Axiom Space (orbital station modules), Intuitive Machines (Nova-C lunar landers, IM-1 success Feb 2024 and IM-2 in March 2025), and Collins Aerospace (Artemis xEMU spacesuits). Horizontal-launch authorization is currently theoretical; the site mainly functions as a manufacturing and mission-control hub.

sites.name: Midland International Air & Space Port
sites.activity: Commercial airport with horizontal-launch licence. Anchor tenant XCOR Aerospace (Lynx) folded in 2017; the Midland Development Corp continues to court replacement spaceflight tenants but no orbital launches have flown. Delta restored daily Austin service in April 2024; Southwest dominates passenger ops alongside regional carriers.

sites.name: SpaceX Starbase (Boca Chica)
sites.activity: SpaceX's Starship/Super Heavy development and orbital test site; incorporated as a Texas city in May 2025. Two operational Orbital Launch Mounts (OLM-1, OLM-2) plus Massey's test-stand complex. Through May 2026, Integrated Flight Tests have stepped through tower-catch booster recovery and orbital reentry; current cadence is roughly one IFT per 8–12 weeks. Adjacent Roberts Road yard fabricates the rapid-iteration airframes.

sites.name: Blue Origin Launch Site One (Corn Ranch)
sites.activity: 165,000-acre private spaceport 30 miles north of Van Horn. New Shepard suborbital tourism and research vehicle flies from this complex; cadence reached a monthly rhythm by NS-37/NS-38 in January 2026. Site also includes BE-3U, BE-4 and BE-7 engine test cells supporting the New Glenn and Blue Moon programs flown elsewhere.

sites.name: Mid-Atlantic Regional Spaceport (MARS)
sites.activity: Co-located with NASA Wallops on Virginia's Eastern Shore. Pad 0A, currently being upgraded for Antares 330/Eclipse, has flown Antares 230+/Cygnus ISS resupply (CRS-2) since 2019. Pad 0B supports Northrop Grumman Minotaur. Launch Complex 2 hosts Rocket Lab Electron (active since Jan 2023). Launch Complex 3 is being built for Rocket Lab's Neutron, first flight expected 2025–2026.

sites.name: Wallops Flight Facility
sites.activity: NASA's principal sounding-rocket range, also the home of orbital ops at MARS (above). Wallops conducts ~20 sounding-rocket and target launches per year (Black Brant, Terrier-Improved Malemute) plus airborne science from its 8,750 ft runway. Mobile range assets deploy globally. Rocket Lab Neutron's purpose-built pad came online August 2025.

sites.name: Huntsville International Air & Space Port
sites.activity: First U.S. commercial airport licensed as a reentry site. License is specific to Sierra Space's Dream Chaser, which is intended to land on the 12,600 ft runway after ISS cargo missions; Tenacity's first flight slipped from 2024 into the late-2025/2026 window. The airport otherwise operates as a normal regional airport for Delta/American/United.
